summary: The Council's first session was consumed by the war against the Undying Scorpion. Despite the horrors haunting Konfurtah, Serena pressed the army onward without burying its dead, and when terror spread through the ranks at the news, the Prior Mother's Silver Mallet was revived under the Cult's banner to fight back. At home, the Council shattered Serena's own statue to placate Mhuir, chose the refined Markiavel over a rowdier rival to write the royal anthem, spared Wylio a harsher levy, and approved the controversial Askadar Academy over traditionalist objections.

dilemmas.title: An Anthem Too Refined for the Tavern
dilemmas.prompt: The Tribune has uncovered a shocking oversight: the Council has yet to choose an official anthem for Queen Serena! We must now decide who shall compose this vital piece of royal heritage. To celebrate the Queen's might in war—from the fall of Kauppias to the campaigns in Konfurtah—we could call upon Turin Cauldron, known as the Bardarian, a bold minstrel famed for his aggressive melodies and brazen lyrics. Alternatively, to honor Her Majesty's wit and political brilliance, we might choose Markiavel, a composer renowned for his methodic structures and delicate harmonies—said to be moving enough to make a god weep. Shall we entrust the composition of the Queen's anthem to Turin the Bardarian?

resolution.narrative: Markiavel's anthem is unveiled before the court—an elegant masterpiece of subtle harmonies and rich symbolism. Nobles weep: the anthem embraces all that Serena is—savant, unyielding, smart and beautiful. Yet among the common folk, the anthem falls flat: too quiet, too clever to be sung by drunkards in taverns.

dilemmas.title: A Statue Sacrificed for Peace
dilemmas.prompt: During the Age of Disorder, we outlawed the Orange Pearls of the Siar Ocean—Mhuir's national treasure—in hopes of repairing relations with our neighbor. Now, the Royal Artisan has crafted the first official statue of Queen Serena, destined for display in the Royal Palace. To capture Serena's golden eyes, he used Orange Pearls. When the Mhuirian diplomat Euphos learned of this, he expressed deep offense, calling the statue an insult to Mhuir's proud history. The statue is scheduled to be placed in the central garden of the Royal Palace as planned—but we could choose to destroy it and order a new statue to avoid a potential diplomatic incident. Shall we destroy the Queen's statue to avoid offending Mhuir?

resolution.narrative: The statue is shattered, forever hidden from public view. Euphos departs appeased, and relations with Mhuir remain steady. A new artisan is commissioned for a less controversial piece—but the result is dull, lacking the brilliance of the one adorned with Orange Pearls. Reluctantly, Serena accepts our choice, though she grumbles about foreign sensibilities shaping her eternal image.

dilemmas.title: Tradition Spares the Young
dilemmas.prompt: An important religious tradition forbids people below 13 to carry weapons, because children are sacred to the Mother and should be protected from violence. Queen Serena suggests that repealing this tradition will allow us to dramatically increase the recruits for our army, but the Prior Mother begs us to consider the effects on the fathers and the mothers and the morale of all Ankist. Do we allow the recruitment of minors?

resolution.narrative: Our kids are the future of the kingdom, and 'tis not by chance that our traditions preserve them. The fathers and mothers of all Ankist breathe a sigh of relief, hoping the conflict in the South will end soon.

dilemmas.title: Onward, Leaving the Dead Behind
dilemmas.prompt: The scorching heat and the constant losses are draining our troops. Serena wants to advance headlong towards Nidar Ilak, but our scouts report that the Geedka Oasis to the east, towards the mountains, may hold the missing corpses of many of our soldiers, unburied and severed from the reincarnation cycle. Although we should not base our military strategies on beliefs, rumors about their terrible fate weigh heavily. However, reaching Geedka to bury them would also slow our advance. Do we divert our army to give our fallen a proper burial?

resolution.narrative: While the Mother rightly commands us to respect the dead, our duties demand even more care for winning the war and the sake of the living. For this reason, we make preparations to advance on the main front. We won't be able to come back and bury our dead later if we lose the war.

dilemmas.title: Faith Leads the Silver Mallet
dilemmas.prompt: Our spies confirm the fears of our troops: Scorpion's soldiers possess the ability to extract blood from human bodies, creating a fluid enabling survival in the desert without food and water. To counter Widram's unnatural menace, our troops demand the return of the Silver Mallet, an elite force made of both the Cult and the army, effective against such supernatural adversaries. This force has always been traditionally led by the Cult, so the Prior Mother vehemently insists we put a Sister to lead the Mallet, prioritizing spiritual guidance. But we could instead have it guided by a military exponent for easier control. Should we heed the Prior Mother's request?

resolution.narrative: A holy elite force aimed at fighting unnatural abominations is exactly what we need now, if only for morale. Under the Cult's control, the Silver Mallet is re-established to join our ranks and help us in our fight against the blood-drinking demons of the Undying Scorpion.

dilemmas.title: Wylio Spared the Greater Levy
dilemmas.prompt: Wylio has an unacceptable rate of deserters in our army. It is common opinion that this is due to their spineless citizens, who care only for money instead of the glory of Ankist. Some think they should compensate the rest of the Kingdom by providing us more soldiers, to make up for the likely deserters. Others claim instead we shouldn't punish all Wylians for a bunch of pavid compatriots, as Wylio already provides as much as all other duchies. Do we raise the levy in Wylio?

resolution.narrative: It is unfair for a duchy to pay more than is requested of the others. The levy remains untouched.

dilemmas.title: The Askadar Academy Opens Its Doors
dilemmas.prompt: Despite the burning indignation of Ankist's traditionalists, the influx of Enkhali immigrants in our kingdom has sparked a wave of curiosity among progressive nobles. Fascinated by northern customs, some have taken a particular interest in the martial art of Askadar, recognizing its deadly precision as invaluable for modern warfare. They propose funding an academy to teach the art to non-Enkhali, believing it could revolutionize our military. However, conservative nobles fiercely oppose this bastardization of Ankist's martial traditions, warning that this could lead to Enkhali influence over our armed forces. Should we proceed with funding the Askadar academy?

resolution.narrative: We approve the founding of the Askadar Academy. Though traditionalist critics complain, progressive nobles and soldiers are drawn to its brutal elegance. Enkhali masters teach in silence, and over time, their discipline could reshape our ways of war.
